title: Functions defined by definite integrals (accumulation functions)
description: Use a graph to evaluate functions defined by integrals.
topic_path: The fundamental theorem of calculus and accumulation functions
Functions defined by definite integrals (accumulation functions)

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph of function $f$ follows. Let $g(x)=\displaystyle \int_{-2}^x f(t) \, dt$.

![Function f is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 5 to 5. The graph is a set of line segments. The set starts at (negative 5, 2), moves upward to (negative 2, 4), moves horizontally to the right to (1, 4), moves upward to (3, 6), moves downward and ends at (5, 2).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b2d11b8bf1e74d419cb775bd377688b4ea7235dd)

**Evaluate $g(2)$.**

$g(2)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
We define function $g$ using a definite integral of function $f$. For every $x$-value, we calculate $g(x)$ by taking the definite integral of $f$ from $-2$ to $x$.

So to calculate $g(\goldD 2)$ we need to take the definite integral of $f$ from $-2$ to $\goldD{2}$:

$g(\goldD{2})=\displaystyle\int_{-2}^{\goldD 2}f(t)\,dt$

This represents the net area between the graph of $f(t)$ and the $x$-axis from $x=-2$ to $x=2$.

We can find $\displaystyle\int_{-2}^{\goldD 2}f(t)\,dt$ using geometric area formulas.

![The graph of function f has the region between the segments and the x-axis, between x = negative 2 and x = 2, shaded. The segment at the top of the shaded region starts at (negative 2, 4), moves horizontally to the right to (1, 4), then moves upward to (2, 5).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/49e199a032ccc251288bad22b5d5af4a6c88a4dd)

We have a $4$ by $4$ square and a right triangle that occupies half a unit square, so in total the area is $16.5$.
In conclusion, $g(2)=16.5$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph of function $g$ is shown below. Let $h(x)=\displaystyle \int_{-1}^x g(t) \, dt$.

![Function g is graphed. The x-axis goes from negative 5 to 5. The graph is a set of line segments. The set starts at (negative 5, 2), moves upward to (negative 1, 5), moves horizontally to the right to (1, 5), moves downward to (4, 2), moves upward and ends at (5, 7).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b9a3c211ca5295790773536f0cd462cc98a0a92c)

**Evaluate $h(3)$.**

$h(3)=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
We define function $h$ using a definite integral of function $g$. For every $x$-value, we calculate $h(x)$ by taking the definite integral of $g$ from $-1$ to $x$.

So to calculate $h(\goldD{3})$ we need to take the definite integral of $g$ from $-1$ to $\goldD{3}$:

$h(\goldD{3})=\displaystyle\int_{-1}^{\goldD{3}}g(t)\,dt$

This represents the net area between the graph of $g(t)$ and the $x$-axis from $x=-1$ to $x=3$.

We can find $\displaystyle\int_{-1}^{\goldD{3}}g(t)\,dt$ using geometric area formulas.

![The graph of function g has the region between the segments and the x-axis, between x = negative 1 and x = 3, shaded. The segment at the top of the shaded region starts at (negative 1, 5), moves rightward to (1, 5), then moves downward to (3, 3).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bd4489093d997b8e0d71d0cf2b09e067f072e75b)

We have a $4$ by $5$ rectangle less a $2$ by $2$ right triangle, so in total the area is $18$.
In conclusion, $h(3)=18$.
